Biodiversity and Conservation
https://doi.org/10.1007/s10531-025-03133-7

ORIGINAL RESEARCH



Riparian zones offset community decline in true flies
(Diptera) after lowland rainforest conversion to smallholder
cash crops in Sumatra

Ulfa Ulinnuha1,2 ¬∑ Tamara R. Hartke2,3 ¬∑ Purnama Hidayat1 ¬∑
Damayanti Buchori1,4 ¬∑ Stefan Scheu2,5 ¬∑ Jochen Drescher2,6

Received: 2 April 2025 / Revised: 20 July 2025 / Accepted: 28 July 2025
¬© The Author(s) 2025, corrected publication 2025


Abstract
Tropical land-use change is a major driver of global biodiversity loss, yet its effects on
flies (Diptera) remain underexplored, particularly in Southeast Asia. The present study
addresses this knowledge gap by comparing Diptera abundance, diversity and community
composition between lowland rainforest and smallholder monocultures of rubber and oil
palm in lowland Sumatra, both on riparian soils as well as upland, well-drained soils.
6589 Diptera specimens were collected by canopy fogging from 36 research plots (eight
per land use) in two landscapes‚ÄĒ‚ÄėBukit Duabelas‚Äô and ‚ÄėHarapan‚Äô‚ÄĒin Jambi Province,
Sumatra, Indonesia. Based on 5907 specimens from the ten most abundant families (Ce-
cidomyiidae, Ceratopogonidae, Chironomidae, Chloropidae, Dolichopodidae, Drosophi-
lidae, Limoniidae, Milichiidae, Phoridae, Sciaridae, sorted to 706 morphospecies from
29 subfamilies and 96 genera), we found that average Diptera abundance did not differ
across land uses, but diversity was higher in lowland rainforest compared to monocul-
tures, and community composition different. Patterns varied among families, with some
affected by land use and others by landscape. Irrespective of land use, riparian zones in
Harapan consistently showed higher Diptera abundance and diversity than upland zones,
alongside differing community composition, primarily driven by increased abundances
and diversity of Chironomidae and Dolichopodidae. These insights highlight that tropical
rainforest conversion negatively affects Diptera populations, depending on landscape con-
text and observed family, and that riparian zones can support greater Diptera abundance
and diversity than upland zones. Overall, protecting and restoring riparian zones to buffers
would thus likely benefit conservation and sustainability efforts in Southeast Asian cash
crop agriculture.

Keywords Land-use change ¬∑ Riparian buffer zones ¬∑ Rubber ¬∑ Oil palm ¬∑ Southeast asia




Communicated by Nigel Stork.

Extended author information available on the last page of the article


                                                                                 13
                                                                     Biodiversity and Conservation


Introduction

Tropical rainforests are among the most biodiverse ecosystems on this planet (Myers et al.
2000), a their loss and fragmentation due to increasing human land use constitute the major
drivers of tropical biodiversity loss (Gibson et al. 2011). While the conversion of natural
ecosystems typically results in biodiversity loss, the levels of biodiversity sustained varies
both among and within agricultural systems (Oakley and Bicknell 2022; Wenzel et al. 2024).
Furthermore, the impact of land-use change on animal biodiversity not only differs between
major taxa (Magurran et al. 2018; Deere et al. 2022) but also within large taxonomic units
(Wagner et al. 2021; Kasmiatun et al. 2023). This highlights the need to assess changes in
biodiversity due to tropical land-use change considering both heterogeneity within land
uses (e.g., management, geology and climate) as well as ecological heterogeneity within
and among response taxa.
   Diptera (flies) are among the most speciose and abundant animals. They contribute more
than 10% of all known animal species (almost 180,000 of 1.58 million; GBIF.org 2025) and
there are up to 17 million flies per each human on Earth (McAlister 2018). The crane flies
alone (Tipulidae sensu lato, or Tipuloidea) have more than two and a half times as many
described species as all mammals combined ‚ąí 15,566 versus 6,292 (Oosterbroek 2025), and
nine further Diptera families rival or surpass the global diversity of mammals: Muscidae
(> 6150 spp.), Syrphidae (> 6900 spp.), Asilidae and Ceratopogonidae (> 7000 spp. each),
Cecidomyiidae (> 7200 spp.), Dolichopodidae (> 7800 spp.), Chironomidae (> 8700 spp.),
as well as the Limoniidae and Tachinidae (> 10000 spp. each) (B√°nki et al. 2025). In cur-
rently described global species numbers, Diptera are only surpassed by Coleoptera (beetles;
ca. 400,000 species) and rivalled by Lepidoptera (moths and butterflies; ca. 184,000 spe-
cies) and Hymenoptera (ants, bees, wasps and sawflies; ca. 150,000 species) (Brown et al.
2018). In fact, detailed biodiversity studies in Britain and Germany suggest that when tiny
and fragile flies and wasps are included, Diptera and Hymenoptera are considerably more
diverse in Europe than Coleoptera (de Jong 2025). This observation points to a substantial
underestimation of Dipteran diversity in the tropics and subtropics and underscores their
role as likely the least taxonomically studied among the ‚Äėbig four‚Äô most speciose animal
orders on our planet (Brown et al. 2018). There likely exist 400,000‚Äď800,000 species of
Diptera and up to 5‚Äď20% of all animal species may be flies (Marshall 2012). Due to the
huge discrepancy between described and estimated global richness, Diptera are considered
‚Äėdark taxa‚Äô (Srivathsan et al. 2023), i.e. species groups that due to small body size and high
diversity suffer from extreme taxonomic neglect.
   In stark contrast to the large knowledge gaps on the taxonomy and biology of Diptera,
they have enormous influence on human livelihood. They transmit diseases that infect mil-
lions of humans each year, but also contribute to the pollination of plants, biological control
of pest species, are food for vertebrate and invertebrate predators, and processing of dung,
carrion and other organic matter in almost all biomes of our planet, among others (Marshall
2012; Courtney et al. 2017; McAlister 2018). As other animal taxa, flies are severely threat-
ened by land-use change as they are rich in species with specific requirements for micro-
habitat, breeding-site or food resources; Diptera are thus increasingly used as indicators of
habitat quality, in conservation planning and or for comparisons between natural and human
land-use systems (Courtney et al. 2017).




13
Biodiversity and Conservation


    Indonesia forms the major archipelago in Southeast Asia and represents the biodiversity
hotspots of Sundaland and Wallacea (Myers et al. 2000). Here, the production of globally
relevant commodities such as rubber and oil palm is soaring, with currently almost 3 million
hectares of rubber and ca. 17 million of hectares of oil palm (BPS 2024a, b). The rubber
and oil palm boom in Indonesia led to large-scale deforestation in the past decades (Grass
et al. 2020), with record-breaking 0.84 Mha in 2012, mainly in Sumatra and Kalimantan
(Indonesian Borneo) (Margono et al. 2014). Jambi Province in central Sumatra contributes
about 10% and 5% of Indonesia‚Äôs rubber and oil palm production, respectively (BPS 2024a,
b), with almost all of the rubber production and about half of the oil palm production being
controlled by smallholders (BPS 2024c). In total, Jambi Province has lost almost 2 million
hectares of tree cover (43%) between 2001 and 2023 (via Global Forest Watch: Hansen et
al. 2013).
    In Jambi, the land-use change from lowland rainforest to rubber and oil palm has led to
substantial biodiversity loss (often > 50%) across taxa (Barnes et al. 2014; Drescher et al.
2016; Grass et al. 2020), including trees and epiphytes (B√∂hnert et al. 2016; Rembold et
al. 2017), birds (Prabowo et al. 2016) and ants, beetles, butterflies, spiders, springtails and
parasitoid wasps (Nazarreta et al. 2020; Panjaitan et al. 2020; Azhar et al. 2022; Mawan et
al. 2022; Ramos et al. 2022; Kasmiatun et al. 2023). These biodiversity losses are associ-
ated with altered functional compositions of arthropod communities (Potapov et al. 2020;
Junggebauer et al. 2021; Susanti et al. 2021; Kreider et al. 2021), in turn affecting multi-
trophic diversity and ecosystem functioning (Barnes et al. 2017; Pollierer et al. 2023; Pota-
pov et al. 2024). In the present study, we focus on the true flies, Diptera, and compare their
communities in lowland rainforest to those in smallholder monocultures of rubber and oil
palm. Based on patterns of other arthropods from the same collection (Nazarreta et al. 2020;
Panjaitan et al. 2020; Azhar et al. 2022; Mawan et al. 2022; Ramos et al. 2022; Kasmiatun
et al. 2023), we expected overall Diptera abundance and diversity in lowland rainforest
to be higher than in monocultures, and community composition different among land-use
systems. However, as previous research established that responses of beetles (Coleoptera)
to the conversion of rainforest into rubber and oil palm monocultures differ between bee-
tle families (Kasmiatun et al. 2023), we also explore community decline among Diptera
families.
    Riparian zones in plantation systems are increasingly recognized as areas of high conser-
vation value. Guidelines from the Roundtable of Sustainable Oil Palm (RSPO) recommend
excluding planting in riparian buffers and promoting the ‚Äúmaintenance and/or enhance-
ment of [‚Ä¶] riparian buffers‚ÄĚ as ‚Äúidentified protection areas‚ÄĚ (RSPO 2021). This is because
riparian buffers, composed of forest or non-production plants, serve as microclimate refuge
areas in oil palm landscapes (Williamson et al. 2021; Deere et al. 2022). They help mitigate
the loss of arthropod and freshwater species following forest clearing (Deere et al. 2022;
Rojas-Castillo et al. 2024) and enhance the economic viability and longevity of floodplain
plantations by countering erosion (Horton et al. 2018). In Jambi and other regions of Indo-
nesia, however, forested buffer zones in cash crops are sparse, as smallholder and industrial
plantations of both rubber and oil palm often extend directly to the edge of water bodies
(J. Drescher, pers. obs.). While it has been demonstrated that riparian buffers of mature
oil palm exhibit slightly different environmental conditions and ecosystem functions (e.g.,
reduced herbivory) compared to non-riparian areas of the plantation (Woodham et al. 2019),
it remains unclear how plantations within riparian zones (actively harvested and fertilized


                                                                                   13
                                                                                     Biodiversity and Conservation


right up to the water‚Äôs edge, with no buffer) differ from non-riparian plantations in their
capacity to support arthropod biodiversity. Therefore, in addition to comparing Diptera
communities across three land uses (forest, rubber and oil palm), we also compare Diptera
communities between these land uses in riparian zones and those in non-riparian (‚Äúupland‚ÄĚ)
zones. In previous analyses (J. Drescher, unpubl. data), we observed no significant dif-
ferences in overall canopy arthropod abundance, as well as ant genus richness and com-
munity composition, between upland and riparian zones in rainforest, rubber and oil palm.
Consequently, we hypothesized that there will be no differences in the abundance, richness
and community composition of Diptera communities between upland and riparian habitats
across forest, rubber and oil palm plantations.


Materials and methods

Study region and design

The present study was conducted in the framework of the German-Indonesian collaborative
research project ‚ÄúEFForTS‚ÄĚ (‚ÄėEcological and socioeconomic functions of lowland rainfor-
est transformation systems in Jambi, Sumatra, Indonesia; ‚Äčh‚Äčt‚Äčt‚Äčp‚Äčs‚Äč:‚Äč/‚Äč/‚Äčw‚Äčw‚Äčw‚Äč.‚Äču‚Äčn‚Äči‚Äč-‚Äčg‚Äčo‚Äče‚Äčt‚Äčt‚Äči‚Äčn‚Äčg‚Äče‚Äčn‚Äč.‚Äčd‚Äče‚Äč/‚Äč
e‚Äčn‚Äč/‚Äč31‚Äč ‚Äč0‚Äč9‚Äč9‚Äč5.‚Äč‚Äčh‚Äčt‚Äčm‚Äčl‚Äč‚Äč‚Äč)‚Äč ‚Äč. Since 2012, the EFForTS project studies the drivers and consequences
of rainforest conversion to rubber and oil palm plantations in Jambi Province, Sumatra,
Indonesia, using a replicated plot design in and around two forest reserves (henceforth
referred to as ‚Äėlandscapes‚Äô), i.e. the conservation area ‚ÄėBukit Duabelas National Park‚Äô and
the reforestation concession ‚ÄėHarapan Rainforest‚Äô (https://hutanharapan.id/en/) (Drescher et
al. 2016). Four plots each in lowland rainforest as well as smallholder plantations of rubber
and oil palm were established in well-drained, upland zones in both landscapes, resulting in
4 plots ‚®Į 3 land uses ‚®Į 2 landscapes = 24 plots. Each plot measured 50 m ‚®Į 50 m, contain-
ing marked subplots for coordinated sampling and a meteostation in the centre (Drescher
et al. 2016). In the Harapan landscape, four additional plots of each of the three land uses
were established in riparian zones, i.e. areas near streams and rivers which lay between the
low and high watermarks, were frequent subject to flooding, with high groundwater tables
and characteristic ‚Äėmottles‚Äô in soil indicating waterlogging (resulting in 24 ‚Äėupland‚Äô + 12
‚Äėriparian‚Äô = 36 plots; Fig. 1). Riparian zones in plantations were areas of productive rubber
trees and oil palms right to the edge of the water body, and of a similar age cohort as the
corresponding upland plantation sites (12‚Äď21 years for rubber and 13‚Äď20 years for oil palm
at the time of sample collection). They did not constitute ‚Äėriparian buffers‚Äô, i.e. containing
trees and shrubs for conservation purposes or filtering of nutrient leaching.

Sampling and Identification

Diptera samples were collected between 03 June and 28 August 2017 using canopy fogging
from three target canopies (‚Äėfogging subplots‚Äô) per each of the 36 plots in upland and ripar-
ian zones of (1) rainforest and monocultures of (2) rubber and (3) oil palm (Fig. 2). No signs
of recent herbicide use or fertilization were observed in rubber or oil palm plots. Fogging
subplots were selected 30 m apart in a rough triangle around the plot centre avoiding canopy
gaps and fallen trees. In each of the fogging subplots, 50 mL DECIS 25 EC (Bayer Crop Sci-


13
Biodiversity and Conservation




Fig. 1 Design and location of 36 EFForTS research plots in lowland rainforest (green), smallholder oil
palm monocultures (red), and smallholder rubber monoculture (yellow) in Jambi Province, Sumatra, In-
donesia. The 12 plots in the western Bukit Duabelas landscape were established on well-drained, upland
soils, while plots in the Harapan landscape were established both on well-drained upland soils (squares)
as well as riparian, waterlogged soils (triangles).(modified from Ramos et al. 2022)


ence, 25 g/L deltamethrine) mixed in four liters of petroleum oil (‚Äúwhite oil‚ÄĚ) were applied
to the canopy using a Swingfog SN50 (Swingtec GmbH, Isny, Germany). Underneath each
target canopy, eight conical collection traps measuring 1 m ‚®Į 1 m were placed. Each trap
was fitted with a 250 mL polyethylene flasks filled with 100 mL of 96% ethanol. Two
hours after application of the insecticide, the eight flasks of each subplot were removed.
The arthropod mix of each target canopy was first combined into a single sample, but sub-
sequently split into two halves, first visually and by fine scale up to the nearest 0.1 g. One
half was reserved for analysis at G√∂ttingen University (‚ÄėA series‚Äô), while the other was used
for analysis in IPB University (‚ÄėB series‚Äô). Both A and B series were sorted to orders for
subsequent analysis and stored at ‚ąí20 ¬įC in 99.8% EtOH. Arthropod abundances per plot
are highly correlated between the two series across arthropod orders (J. Drescher, unpubl.
data) and even within an ant genus (Ehlers et al. 2024), suggesting that taxonomic patterns
between the series are highly similar. Hence, we here focus on 6589 Diptera specimens
of the B series only. After sorting to families, 5907 specimens of the ten most abundant
families were further sorted to subfamilies, genera, species and morphospecies using an
Olympus SZ61 stereo microscope and identification keys as well as taxonomic information
in the ‚ÄėManual of Nearctic Diptera‚Äô (McAlpine et al. 1981), ‚ÄėFlies‚ÄĒThe Natural History and
Diversity of Diptera‚Äô (Marshall 2012) and ‚ÄėManual of Afrotropical Diptera‚Äô (Kirk-Spriggs
and Sinclair 2017).




                                                                                              13
                                                                                 Biodiversity and Conservation




Fig. 2 Upland and riparian lowland rainforest, and rubber and oil palm plantations. While both types of
zones receive high levels of rain, ‚Äėupland‚Äô zones as defined for this study are areas on well-drained soils
where above- and belowground water runoff is high, and stagnant water is rarely encountered. Plots in
‚Äėriparian‚Äô zones are situated near creeks and streams which spill over during rainy seasons, leaving high
ground water tables and stagnant surface water for weeks or months each year. Note that ‚Äėriparian zones‚Äô
as used here are not ‚Äėriparian buffers‚Äô, i.e. of different plants than the planted crop. Instead, ‚Äėriparian
zones‚Äô in this study are rainforest and active plantations extending right to the banks


Statistical analysis

All statistical analyses were performed in R version 4.3.2 (R Core Team 2023), with code
adapted from previous EFForTS related publications (Junggebauer et al. 2021; Ramos et al.
2022; Kasmiatun et al. 2023); Ehlers et al. 2024) and visualized with ‚Äėggplot2‚Äô (Wickham
2016). Observed and estimated Hill numbers were calculated for q = 0, q = 1 and q = 2, cor-
responding to richness, Shannon and Simpson diversity, using the package ‚ÄėiNEXT‚Äô (Hsieh


13
Biodiversity and Conservation


et al. 2016). A series of Generalized Linear Models (GLM) was used to analyze data in two
approaches:
    First, the response variables ‚Äėabundance per plot‚Äô, ‚Äėrichness‚Äô (Hill q = 0) and ‚Äėdiversity‚Äô
(exponent of Shannon entropy, Hill q = 1; inverse Simpson diversity, Hill q = 2), and com-
munity composition were compared among the predictor variables ‚Äėland use‚Äô (forest, rubber,
oil palm), ‚Äėlandcape + zone‚Äô (Bukit Duabelas upland, Harapan upland, Harapan riparian),
and their interaction using data from all 36 plots. Second, focusing on the Harapan land-
scape where an even design of 12 plots in upland zones and 12 plots in riparian zones across
three land uses were established, the response variables ‚Äėabundance per plot‚Äô, ‚Äėrichness‚Äô
(Hill q = 0) and ‚Äėdiversity‚Äô (exponent of Shannon entropy, Hill q = 1; inverse Simpson diver-
sity, Hill q = 2), and community composition were compared among the predictor variables
‚Äėland use‚Äô (rainforest, rubber, oil palm), ‚Äėzone‚Äô (riparian, upland) and their interaction. Full
models including the interaction between ‚Äėland-use‚Äô and ‚Äėlandscape + zone‚Äô (first approach:
12 plots each in Bukit Duabelas upland, Harapan upland, Harapan riparian), or ‚Äėland use‚Äô
and ‚Äėzone‚Äô (second approach: 12 plots each in Harapan upland, Harapan riparian) were
simplified to the most parsimonious model based on AICc (BartoŇĄ 2024). ANOVA was
used to test for significant deviation from the null model, after checking model fit with
DHARMa (Hartig 2022). Diptera community composition was compared among ‚Äėland-use‚Äô
and ‚Äėlandscape + zone‚Äô (first approach, 36 plots) or ‚Äėland use‚Äô and ‚Äėzone‚Äô (second approach,
24 plots) using multivariate glm (Wang et al. 2021), and visualized using a latent variable
model in boral (bayesian ordination and regression analysis; Hui 2016) and gg-boral (Bed-
ward 2020). To compare this very sensitive, model-based approach to more common, less
sensitive, distance-based approaches, community composition was additionally compared
among ‚Äėland-use‚Äô and ‚Äėlandscape + zone‚Äô (first approach, 36 plots) or ‚Äėland use‚Äô and ‚Äėzone‚Äô
(second approach, 24 plots) using multivariate analysis of variance (MANOVA) and visu-
alized using non-metric multidimensional scaling (NMDS; vegan::mds; Bray-Curtis dis-
similarity). Ranked abundances of Diptera communities were prepared using RankAbund
(Hartke 2020) and Venn diagrams of species overlap between habitats and land uses were
calculated using ‚Äėvegan‚Äô, and visualized with the help of a flash tool for proportional circles
in Venn diagrams on https://www.stefanjol.nl/venny. See the full R project including data
and code here: https://doi.org/10.25625/8P0YEJ.


Results

In the dry season 2017, 6589 canopy Diptera specimens were collected as part of the B
series from 36 EFForTS research plots. A total of 5907 of the Diptera specimens belonged
to ten families, representing 89.6% of the Diptera community: Ceratopogonidae (N = 1666;
25.3%), Chironomidae (N = 1017; 15.4%), Phoridae (N = 822; 12.5%), Cecidomyiidae
(N = 604; 9.2%), Milichiidae (N = 543; 8.2%), Sciaridae (N = 481; 7.3%), Chloropidae
(N = 261; 4%), Dolichopodidae (N = 223; 3.4%), Drosophilidae and Limoniidae (for both
N = 145; 2.2%). These 5907 specimens were further sorted to 706 morphospecies from 96
genera and 29 subfamilies (Table S1). Forcipomyiinae, Ceratopogoninae, Metopininae,
Chironominae and Phyllomyzinae were the most abundant subfamilies with more than 500
individuals each, while Gaurax, Paramyia, Forcipomyia, Drosophila, Stilobezzia and Culi-




                                                                                    13
                                                                                 Biodiversity and Conservation


coides were the most diverse genera with more than 20 morphospecies each (Table S1, for
photos see https://ecotaxonomy.org/ and here: https://doi.org/10.25625/8P0YEJ).
   Upland zones in the Bukit Duabelas landscape contributed 75% of the overall species
pool and 30% of the species pool were exclusively found there (Fig. S2A). By contrast,
Harapan upland zones contributed about half of the species (6% exclusive) while Harapan
riparian about 60% of the species pool (11% exclusive) (Fig. S2A). Across upland and ripar-
ian zones, rainforest contributed more than 75% of the overall species pool (30% exclusive),
while rubber and oil palm combined contributed about half of the species pool (Fig. S2B).
   In the full dataset including four plots each of rainforest, rubber and oil palm in upland
Bukit Duabelas, upland Harapan as well as riparian Harapan, average Diptera abundance
across land uses was highest in Bukit Duabelas upland (N = 205.8 ¬Ī 93.8 per plot), low-
est in Harapan upland (N = 111.7 ¬Ī 33.7 per plot) and intermediate in Harapan riparian
(N = 174.8 ¬Ī 73.8 per plot; all values means ¬Ī sd; Fig. 3). Land use did not influence overall
average Diptera abundance (Table S3), but patterns differed between individual families
(Table S4, A‚ÄďJ): In Chironomidae, Dolichopodidae and Milichiidae, average abundance
was only affected by plot cluster (i.e. Bukit upland, Harapan upland, Harapan riparian, or
‚ÄúLSZON‚ÄĚ), while in Chloropidae, Drosophilidae, Limoniidae and Phoridae, average abun-
dances were influenced only by land use or its interaction with plot cluster. In the remaining
three families, Cecidomyiidae, Ceratopogonidae and Sciaridae, average abundances were
influenced by both plot cluster and land use or their interaction (Table S4).




Fig. 3 Abundance of Diptera sampled by canopy fogging in 36 research plots across three land-use types
(rainforest = green; rubber = orange; oil palm = yellow) in two landscapes (Bukit Duabelas, Harapan) in
Jambi Province, Sumatra, Indonesia (average ¬Ī sd). In the Harapan landscape two zones of water influ-
ence (riparian, upland) were investigated. Test statistics indicate statistically significant influences of
landscape + zone (‚ÄėLSZON‚Äô), land use (‚ÄėLU‚Äô) and their interaction on abundance of Diptera in a series of
GLMs after model simplification (see also Supplementary Tables S3 and S4)


13
Biodiversity and Conservation


   Overall Diptera species richness (Hill q = 0 obs.) followed similar patterns as abundance
(Fig. 4); it was highest in Bukit Duabelas upland plots (S = 99.7 ¬Ī 38.7 per plot), lowest in
Harapan upland plots (S = 66.9 ¬Ī 18 per plot) and intermediate in Harapan riparian plots
(S = 80 ¬Ī 23.7 per plot; values means ¬Ī sd). Land use, however, also significantly influenced
overall Diptera species richness in that within all three plot clusters, rainforest contained
higher species richness than rubber or oil palm (Fig. 4). Beyond observed species richness,
Diptera diversity as estimated richness (Hill q = 0, est.) as well as observed and estimated
(exponential) Shannon entropy (Hill q = 1, obs., est.) and (inverse) Simpson diversity (Hill
q = 2, obs., est.) was also significantly influenced by plot cluster, but also partially by land
use (Table S3). Patterns differed among families, however, with Cecidomyiidae, Cerato-
pogonidae and Chironomidae diversity (including richness) being mainly influenced by
plot cluster, while diversity of Chloropidae, Drosophilidae, Milichiidae and Sciaridae was
mainly influenced by land use. Richness and diversity of Dolichopodidae, Limoniidae and
Phoridae, however, were either partially influenced by land use or the interaction between
plot cluster and land use, or neither (Table S4).
   Overall Diptera community composition across land uses and landscapes was signifi-
cantly influenced both by land use as well as plot cluster (Table S3), as community composi-
tion in rainforest sites were well separated from those in monocultures in multidimensional
analyses, especially those of Bukit Duabelas (Fig. 5). Here, patterns also differed among
families (Table S4A‚ÄďJ, Fig. S5): Community composition of Cecidomyiidae, Ceratopogo-




Fig. 4 Species richness (as observed Hill number 0, or q = 0, or H0obs) of Diptera in three land-use types
(rainforest = green; rubber = orange; oil palm = yellow) in two landscapes (Bukit Duabelas, Harapan) in
Jambi Province, Sumatra, Indonesia (average ¬Ī sd). In the Harapan landscape two zones of water influ-
ence (riparian, upland) were investigated. Test statistics indicate statistically significant influences of land
use (‚ÄėLU‚Äô), landscape + zone (‚ÄėLSZON‚Äô), and their interaction on species richness of Diptera in a series of
GLMs after model simplification (see also Supplementary Tables S3 and S4)


                                                                                                      13
                                                                                   Biodiversity and Conservation


nidae, Dolichopodidae, Milichiidae and Phoridae was influenced by both land use and plot
cluster, or their interaction, while community composition of the Chironomidae and Sciari-
dae was only influenced by land use or its interaction with plot cluster. Community compo-
sition of Chloropidae and Drosophilidae, in turn, was not influenced by either land use or
plot cluster (landscape + zone), while Limoniidae community composition was exclusively
influenced by plot cluster.
    Focusing on the Harapan landscape (four plots each of upland and riparian rainforest,
rubber and oil palm), the collected Diptera community consisted of 3438 Diptera speci-
mens, or ca. 60% of the total collection, from 503 species, 28 subfamilies and 87 genera.
Across land uses, about half of the species pool was shared between upland and riparian
zones, while ca. 30% were exclusively contributed by riparian zones, and ca. 20% by upland
zones, respectively (Fig. S6A). Rainforest contributed more than 65% of the overall species
pool (ca. 23% exclusively), while rubber and oil palm combined contributed about half of
all species to the pool (ca. 25% exclusive) (Fig. S6B). However, patterns differed between
families: In six out of ten families (Cecidomyiidae, Ceratopogonidae, Chironomidae, Doli-
chopodidae, Drosophilidae and Milichiidae), riparian zones contributed more species to
the cumulative Diptera community than upland zones (Fig. S7). In Chloropidae, Phoridae
and Sciaridae species contribution to the overall community was even among riparian and
upland zones, whereas in Limoniidae upland zones contributed more species than riparian
zones (Fig. S7). Rank-abundance curves suggested higher abundances, richness and eve-
ness of Diptera communities in riparian than upland zones for Ceratopogonidae, Chirono-
miodae, Dolichopodidae and Milichiidae but not for the other families (Fig. S8).
    Abundance and richness of the overall Diptera community in the Harapan landscape
was higher in riparian zones (N = 174.8 ¬Ī 73.8, S = 82.7 ¬Ī 22.9 per plot) than in upland zones
(N = 111.7 ¬Ī 33.7, S = 65.3 ¬Ī 18 per plot, means ¬Ī sd), while land use did not affect abundance
or richness (Fig. 6, Table S10). Observed Diptera diversity followed this pattern, with sig-




Fig. 5 Diptera community composition across land uses and water influence zones. A Latent variable
model (LVM) biplot and wald test statistics after manyglm, and B non-metric multidimensional scaling
(NMDS) and Wilks lambda after MANOVA. Land uses (‚ÄúLU‚ÄĚ) include rainforest, rubber and oil palm,
while plot clusters (‚ÄúLSZON‚ÄĚ) include Bukit Duabelas upland, Harapan upland, and Harapan riparian.
Abbreviations specify landscape, land use and zone of a given ellipse with the first capital letter indicating
Bukit Duabelas (‚ÄėB‚Äô) or Harapan (‚ÄėH‚Äô), the capital second letter indicating the land use (‚ÄėF‚Äô= rainforest;
‚ÄėR‚Äô= rubber; ‚ÄėO‚Äô= oil palm) and the lower-case ending indicating zone (‚Äėup‚Äô = upland; ‚Äėrip‚Äô = riparian)


13
Biodiversity and Conservation


nificantly higher values of exponential Shannon entropy (or Hill q = 1, obs.) and inverse
Simpson diversity (or Hill q = 2, obs.) in riparian than upland zones (Fig. S9A‚ÄďC). Fur-
ther, estimated inverse Simpson diversity was significantly higher in riparian than in upland
zones (Fig. S9F), while estimated richness and exponential Shannon entropy were not (Fig.
S9D, E). However, patterns of abundance and diversity between riparian and upland zones
in the Harapan landscape differed among families (Table S11A‚ÄďJ): Chironomidae and Doli-
chopodidae abundance and diversity were largely driven by water influence zone (higher
in riparian zones, lower in upland zones). Abundance and diversity of Chloropidae, Droso-
philidae, Limoniidae and Sciaridae were driven by land use, depending on the measure of
diversity (Table S11). Abundance and diversity of the remainder of families was either not
influenced by land use or zone at all (Cecidomyiidae), or both land use and zone (Phori-
dae: abundance), in only one response variable (Ceratopogonidae: abundance influenced by
zone) or a combination of both (Milichiidae) (Table S11).
    Overall Diptera community composition in the Harapan landscape was significantly
influenced by land use, water influence zone and their interaction (Table S10). Diptera com-
munities in oil palm plantations clustered somewhat separate from those in lowland rainfor-
est or rubber in both a latent variable model as well as non-metric multidimensional scaling,
while communities in upland and riparian zones within land use systems partially separated
as well (Fig. 7). However, patterns differed among the ten Diptera families (Table S11A‚Äď




Fig. 6 Average abundance and richness (¬Ī sd.; as observed Hill number 0, or q = 0, or H0obs) of Diptera
sampled by canopy fogging in 36 research plots across three land-use types (‚ÄėLU‚Äô, rainforest, rubber, oil
palm) in two landscapes (Bukit Duabelas, Harapan) in Jambi Province, Sumatra, Indonesia (average ¬Ī sd).
In the Harapan landscape two zones of water influence (riparian, upland) were investigated. Test statis-
tics indicate significantly higher abundance and richness of Diptera in riparian zones and upland zones
(‚ÄėZON‚Äô) across land uses in GLMs after model simplification. Land use (‚ÄėLU‚Äô) did not influence average
Diptera abundance or richness per plot in the Harapan landscape (see also Supplementary Figure S10 for
estimated richness, observed and estimated Hill numbers q = 1 and q = 2, and Supplementary Tables S11
and S12)


                                                                                               13
                                                                              Biodiversity and Conservation




Fig. 7 Diptera community composition across land uses and water influence zones in the Harapan land-
scape. (A) Latent variable model (LVM) biplot and wald test statistics after manyglm, and (B) non-metric
multidimensional scaling (NMDS) and Wilks lambda after MANOVA. Land uses (‚ÄúLU‚ÄĚ) include rainfor-
est, rubber and oil palm, while water influence zones (‚ÄúZON‚ÄĚ) include waterlogged riparian (‚Äúrip‚ÄĚ) and
well-drained upland (‚Äúup‚ÄĚ). Abbreviations specify landscape (‚ÄėH‚Äô = Harapan), land use (‚ÄėF‚Äô= rainforest;
‚ÄėR‚Äô= rubber; ‚ÄėO‚Äô= oil palm) and zone (‚Äėup‚Äô = upland; ‚Äėrip‚Äô = riparian)

J, Fig. S12A‚ÄďJ), in that community compositions of Cecidomyiidae, Chironomidae and
Milichiidae were only influenced by land use, those of Phoridae and Sciaridae by land use
and its interaction with water influence zone, those of Ceratopogonidae and Dolichopodidae
by both land use and water influence zone, and community composition of Chloropidae,
Drosophilidae and Limoniidae by neither.


Discussion

We examined the differences in Diptera communities within a tropical biodiversity hotspot,
focusing on three prevalent land-use systems: lowland rainforest, and smallholder rubber
and oil palm plantations. Land use significantly influenced overall Diptera abundance and
diversity, with higher levels in rainforest compared to monoculture plantations. Notably,
this varied with landscape, as Diptera abundance and diversity was highest in lowland
rainforest of the Bukit Duabelas landscape. Additionally, within the Harapan landscape we
investigated whether Diptera communities in riparian zones of rainforest, rubber and oil
palm plantations differed from those on upland (well-drained) soils. Results consistently
showed higher Dipteran abundance and diversity in riparian compared to upland zones, but
regardless of land use. This means that, on average, a riparian oil palm plantation may con-
tain higher densities and diversity of Diptera than an upland rainforest, at least with respect
to the reference forest system Harapan Rainforest (https://hutanharapan.id/en/). Community
composition varied between riparian and upland zones, land use also played a significant
role in shaping these differences. Finally, we observed that these patterns were not uni-
form across all Diptera families. Families such as Chloropidae, Drosophilidae, Milichiidae
and Sciaridae best reflected land-use change, while Chironomidae and Dolichopodidae best
reflected the differences between riparian and upland sites in the Harapan landscape.


13
Biodiversity and Conservation


Diptera communities and land use

Smallholder monocultures of rubber and oil palm supported lower density and diversity of
Diptera as well as altered community composition than in rainforest. This finding, consistent
with our first hypothesis, mirrors declines observed in ants (Nazarreta et al. 2020; Rizqulloh
et al. 2021; Kreider et al. 2021; Ehlers et al. 2024), beetles (Kasmiatun et al. 2023), butter-
flies (Panjaitan et al. 2020), spiders (Potapov et al. 2020; Junggebauer et al. 2021; Ramos et
al. 2022), springtails (Susanti et al. 2021; Mawan et al. 2022) and parasitoid wasps (Azhar et
al. 2022) collected from the same sites (Drescher et al. 2016). However, the observed decline
in the number of species of Diptera by ~ 25% was less severe than the ~ 50% decline in the
mentioned taxa. This difference may be attributed to at least three factors: first, flies are
highly mobile and may enter (and leave) plots as ‚Äútourists‚ÄĚ while not actually utilizing nest-
ing or feeding resources in the plot where they were caught, thus possibly overestimating
true Diptera diversity at a site. Second, lower sampling intensity in the present study (eight
traps per subplot, half the specimens sorted to species) compared to the above-cited studies
(sixteen traps, all specimens sorted) likely underestimated species loss, particularly of rare
species in rainforests (and hence, lower species loss detected). Third, the exceptionally high
estimated global Diptera species richness (400.000‚Äď800.000 worldwide; Marshall 2012),
suggests a broader utilization of microhabitats, potentially including those present in rubber
and oil palm plantations, possibly mitigating the impact of rainforest conversion. Neverthe-
less, the marked differences in Diptera abundance, diversity and community composition
between rainforest and monocultures highlight fundamental ecological disparities between
these systems. Key differences likely include habitat space, with rainforest exhibiting over
four times the tree height (and thus, potential habitat) compared to oil palm (Kotowska et al.
2015), and structural complexity, with rainforest characterized by significantly greater tree
density, species richness, understory vegetation and epiphyte abundance than rubber and oil
palm plantations (B√∂hnert et al. 2016; Rembold et al. 2017). Notably, much of the observed
land use effect on Diptera communities was driven by high abundance and diversity of Dip-
tera in the lowland rainforest of the Bukit Duabelas landscape. A similar trend was observed
in overall canopy arthropod abundance and biomass, which was considerably higher in
rainforest of the Bukit Duabelas than the Harapan landscape (Pollierer et al. 2023), whereas
this was not the case in rubber and oil palm plantations. The differences between the two
rainforest systems may be due to differences in soil fertility and landscape diversity (Allen
et al. 2015), with the Bukit Duabelas landscape being characterized by soils with higher clay
content and a more hilly profile the Harapan landscape. Additionally, the differences may
be related to each forest‚Äôs distinct logging history. The Bukit Duabelas rainforest is located
in a mostly undisturbed national park (e.g. Anas et al. 2021), whereas the Harapan rainfor-
est is part of a restoration concession established on selectively logged forest (Harrison and
Swinfield 2015). While both forests exhibit similar broad ecological patterns in terms of
multi-trophic biodiversity (Barnes et al. 2017), multifunctionality (Grass et al. 2020) and
food web structure (Potapov et al. 2024), individual canopy arthropod taxa are consistently
less abundant and diverse in the Harapan than Bukit Duabelas rainforest (ants: Nazarreta
et al. 2020; parasitoid wasps: Azhar et al. 2022; springtails: Mawan et al. 2022; beetles:
Kasmiatun et al. 2023). Combined with our observation that the impact of land-use change
varies among Diptera families, this highlights the important role of site-specific histories




                                                                                   13
                                                                      Biodiversity and Conservation


and ecological contexts in shaping communities of individual taxa, reinforcing the need for
targeted approaches in conservation and restoration efforts.

Diptera communities and riparian zones

To compare Diptera communities between upland and riparian rainforest, and rubber and oil
palm plantations, we focused on the Harapan landscape, where Diptera were collected from
12 upland and 12 riparian sites across the studied three land-use types. Generally, Diptera
were more abundant and diverse in riparian zones than in upland zones. This pattern was
predominantly due to two families, i.e. Chironomidae and Dolichopodidae, who are char-
acteristic of riparian zones, like many aquatic Diptera (Marshall 2012; Adler and Courtney
2019). Chironomidae form part of Nematocera with aquatic larvae and often comprise more
than half of all animal species in lakes and streams (Marshall 2012). Adults of the ca. 8000
described (and many more undescribed) species of Chironomidae are among the most abun-
dant insects, and breed in almost any water body available, from sea shores to hot springs,
glacial runoff and ephemeral waterbodies in landfills and agriculture (Marshall 2012). In our
study, the Chironomidae community mostly comprised (> 50%) the subfamily Chironomi-
nae, known for forming large swarms near lakes, and the genus Polypedilum, known for its
ability to withstand extreme desiccation until rainfall occurs (Marshall 2012). By contrast,
Dolichopodidae (long-legged flies) are empidoid Brachycera, with both adults and larvae
living as predators on collembolans, oligochaetes and other flies. Even though life history
and habitat information is limited to a few of the approximately 7000 known species, Doli-
chopodidae are often associated with aquatic habitats and often breed along lakeshores and
temporary water bodies (Marshall 2012; Adler and Courtney 2019). By contrast, Sciaridae
and Drosophilidae, with their abundance and diversity being similar in upland and ripar-
ian zones, are less dependent on water but rather breed in rotting organic material or in
soil. Consequently, it is the water-dependent Diptera families which are responsible for the
greater abundance and diversity of Diptera in riparian compared to upland areas.
    This observation was in contrast to our expectation, as previous research indicated no
significant differences between upland and riparian zones in overall canopy arthropod
abundance, as well as ant genus richness and community composition, across land uses
(J. Drescher, unpubl. data). Tree canopies are unique habitats, however, and animal and
plant communities inhabiting them may do so regardless of whether the habitat beneath the
canopy is riparian or upland (e.g. Lowman and Nadkarni 1995; Lowman and Rinker 2004).
However, our results align with observations of higher amphibian abundance and richness
in riparian forests, rubber and oil palm plantations compared to upland areas as reported
from the same research sites (Paoletti et al. 2018). Like Chironomidae and Dolichopodidae,
amphibians typically rely on water for breeding, explaining their greater abundance and
diversity in riparian zones. Similar trends have been observed in temperate regions, where
small patches of woody vegetation along streams have more impact on communities of
water-associated taxa like Ephemeroptera (mayflies), Plecoptera (stoneflies) and Trichop-
tera (caddisflies) compared to terrestrial taxa such as spiders and beetles (Forio et al. 2020).
Their increased abundance or biomass entails indirect effects on other taxa, e.g. height-
ened numbers of insectivorous birds near streams (Murakami and Nakano 2002; Chan et
al. 2008), changes in water-adjacent herbivory and aboveground invertebrate food webs
(Henschel et al. 2001; Wesner 2010) or fertilization of the decomposer soil food web (Koro-


13
Biodiversity and Conservation


bushkin et al. 2016). Our findings suggests that riparian zones, whether in natural forest or
plantations and regardless of forest cover, directly influence the abundance and diversity
of water-associated taxa. Frequent flooding may further increase nutrient availability and
energy flows in riparian sites by mixing the terrestrial and riverine food webs (Ballinger
and Lake 2006; Liao et al. 2022), thus potentially leading to increased biodiversity in not
directly water-associated taxa as well.


Conclusions

Our study reveals the intricate and nuanced responses of Diptera communities to land-use
change, landscape context, and environmental conditions. While land-use change remains
the primary driver of Diptera decline‚ÄĒaligning with patterns observed across numerous
taxa‚ÄĒwe also discover a surprising resilience within riparian zones of rainforest and mono-
culture plantations, which harbor higher abundances and diversity than their upland coun-
terparts in the Harapan landscape, irrespective of land use. These findings underscore the
vital importance of riparian zones as refuges for biodiversity and highlight the urgent need
to establish and enforce protected, ideally non-production riparian buffers in Southeast Asia
cash crop agriculture, particularly in Jambi Province. Protecting these critical corridors is
not only essential for sustaining Diptera diversity but also for supporting a wide array of
other animal and plant species that are interconnected through multilayered, multitrophic
food webs‚ÄĒmaking riparian buffers indispensable for overall ecosystem health and resil-
ience in the Southeast Asian tropics.

wrote the first version of the manuscript. All authors contributed to drafts and approved the final version of
